Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs
Feat will learn the constants for the subset of features that are differentiable using gradient descent. It is a local search built within the larger search for feature forms.

Imagine recording a MRI of a brain. A pixel in the resulting image has a single grey value. But since the image resolution is of course limited, this grey value number represents the average physical attributes of a small area of brain tissue.

The pixel size or spacing denotes the sizes of a single pixel, i.e., the width and height of the physical volume from which it is a representation. This is an important attribute of, e.g., satellite and medical images.

Since medical images are often recorded as volumes, we employ the term voxelspacing in MedPy. Note that MedPy aims to be dimensionality independent, hence voxelspacing can mean a volume arbitrary dimensions.

When working with texture features as in your case, the voxelspacing is important to consider to make the resulting features between images comparable. Coarseness should, for example, be expressed in physical units rather than pixels.

So, I experimented a little bit. This post will have two (small) sections. One answering your question and  one summarising what I found.

**Section A:**
 I am not certain about how MATPOWER does it. I know what it says in the manual and what it should be doing according to theory, but I have not gone through the code line by line. 

Here is what should be happening (I will be very explicit just in case someone does not know electrical power system stuff): The voltage get points of the generator should control the voltage amplitude at the bus the generator is connected at. This quantity could be given either in Volts or per units. The MATPOWER manual says that they are using per units, which makes much more sense. 

In other words, the optimum vector of voltage amplitudes produced by the OPF should be used to fill in the voltage set points of the generators. Specifically, the bus ID to which the generator is connected should have the same voltage amplitude as the generator setpoint.

**Section B:**
I found a way to NOT have the PF solver iterate. I do not know if it is the proper solution but it seems to be closer. Right now it is very late where I am and I do not have the time, but tomorrow I will edit this post and include a minimal ingredients solution (this is the first time I am using a platform like this, I do not know what I am saying is good etiquette). To give a small peak, I addressed the voltage stepping issue, just to be sure, and made sure that the voltage angles and power generation (VA in bus and PG QG in gen) are in degrees and MVW, MVar respectively.

Some of the three steps might be redundant. I will check tomorrow.

> That the magnitude is stored as string seems like a bug to me.

@dalito . The first time I saw it, it also seemed strange to me, but now I see it as a feature of pint, not a bug: if you use the `Q_(magnitude, unit) `style of call, it is kind of reasonable that no evaluation is done on magnitude (note that this is different from initializing from a string as in `Q_(string)`). This way you can define Quantities with arbitrary objects for the magnitude (e.g. with your own implementation of base 10 decimal).

Although, I don't think the quotation mark is a match character with *sh*/*bash* by default, and what's a match really depends on the shell and its configuration¹, eg.:

```bash
# bash, note that the error is from `cat` itself, so bash didn't bother with the `?`:
$ cat https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0ZnZMhkUyI
cat: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0ZnZMhkUyI: No such file or directory
# zsh:
% cat https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0ZnZMhkUyI
zsh: no matches found: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O0ZnZMhkUyI
```

It should also work for that one:
```bash
% git diff HEAD^ 
zsh: no matches found: HEAD^
```
with *zsh*'s EXTENDEDGLOB (which is usually nice but for that case).

[1]: of course we can just make a rule that add quotes everywhere it could.

> > From what I've seen, upon launch Will starts 3 new processes - one each for the scheduler, XMPP, and web server (bottle). Normally on `KeyboardInterupt` or `SystemExit`, Will stops each of these threads cleanly.
> > I've been experimenting with running Will under [envconsul](https://github.com/hashicorp/envconsul), which watches and loads configs from the Consul KV store and inserts them into the ENV before starting Will. The docstring is fine, there's not much documentation elsewhere. The most important line in the docs related to your problem is probably this one:

> all other types --> dict with the __getstate__ or vars() of the object

Which basically means that if Serpent encounters an object of a type that doesn't fall in any of the other categories, it does this last resort behavior. Not all types support this last resort though hence Serpent is then giving up and suggesting that you "Give it vars() or an appropriate __getstate__".
Another solution is to not feed such types to Serpent in the first place, and try dealing with more basic types for serialization. Or add custom serialization rules using Serpent's ``register_class`` function.

As you can see in this output, kde_kwallet.so was not built because of the user's home directory. As explained in my first post, this home directory makes kde4-config to produce an error before giving the path to the includes. And this output fails the kde_check() function in extensions.py so kde_kwallet.so is not built :
{{{
#!shell
test@sid:/tmp/keyring-0.2$ kde4-config --install include
trying to create local folder /nonexistant: Permission denied
/usr/include/
test@sid:/tmp/keyring-0.2$
}}}

Keyring should not rely on a specific home directory to be built. I mean, if the user you're using does not have a home directory, it should not stop you to build Keyring. This assumption is widely used, especially in chrooted environments where your goal is just to build the software with minimal dependencies.

Hey @TheTradingWarrior,

Sorry for the (very) late reply! 

I designed the framework with a single price in mind. I was mostly focused on longer-term strategies, where assuming the close price was not an issue. 

In your case, I'm afraid you would have to write some custom code. The ```allocate``` functions on ```StrategyBase``` and ```SecurityBase``` should be good starting points to see how this all works. 

Basically, you have two options. The best one, in my opinion, would be to re-write/extend the core framework to support bid/ask prices (perhaps with a new BidAskStrategyBase class that inherits from StrategyBase). The logic within ```SecurityBase.allocate``` would have to be rewritten to automatically use the bid or ask depending on the direction of the trade. 

Alternatively, you could write a custom Algo that will analyze the direction of the trade, and overwrite the price at that point in time (with the bid or ask price). This would run right before the Rebalance algo. In this case, you wouldn't have to touch the core framework. This is more of a hack, but it could work. 

I hope this makes sense, and if not, please don't hesitate reach out. 

Cheers,
Phil

> oh today it stop working in 3 hours....no error, just stop download.

Hmm, there is a slim possibility that a HTTP requests "gets stuck" and the client waits forever for a reply from the remote server. Some HTTP requests send by gallery-dl - for some reason - don't have a timeout, so it probably happened with one of those. Fixing this should be easy. In the meantime: `Ctrl+c` and try again.

> why i feel it so slow to check downloaded link

Because it has to get download URL and metadata before it can check if a file has already been downloaded (same as youtube-dl). It doesn't help that Sankaku is incredibly slow itself, so you have to wait 2-5 seconds before each HTTP request (to avoid `429 Too Many Requests` errors) and then you have to wait for the request itself to finish, which might take another 5 seconds.

When using sakaku stuff, you should really use the `--range` command-line option when necessary, as it allows the extractor to quickly jump ahead. `gallery-dl --range 250- URL...` is going to immediately jump to image nr. 250 and start from there.

I found a way to create a threaded stream using capture_sequence. below an example for direct grayscale capture:
```


import io
import time
from picamera import PiCamera
import cv2
import numpy
from threading import Thread
import time


w=640
h=480
fw=(w+31)//32*32
fh=(h+15)//16*16
Y=None
camera=PiCamera()
camera.resolution = (w, h)
camera.framerate = 40
camera.exposure_mode='off'
camera.awb_mode='off'
camera.awb_gains=1
camera.shutter_speed=15000
stream_done=False
cv2.namedWindow("detection", cv2.WINDOW_AUTOSIZE)


def diff(frame1,frame2):
    return int(cv2.sumElems(frame1)[0]-cv2.sumElems(frame2)[0])

def outputs():
    global Y
    stream = io.BytesIO()
    while not stream_done:
        
        yield stream

        stream.seek(0)
        Y=numpy.fromstring(stream.getvalue(),dtype=numpy.uint8,count=fw*fh).\
           reshape((fh,fw))
        
        stream.seek(0)
        stream.truncate()

def capt():
    camera.capture_sequence(outputs(), 'yuv', use_video_port=True)
    pass

def startcapt():
    t = Thread(target=capt, args=())
    t.daemon = True
    t.start()

##TEST 
startcapt()
time.sleep(0.5)
diffs=[]
frame2=Y
while cv2.waitKey(1) != ord("q"):
    #cv2.imshow('detection',Y)
    frame1 = frame2
    time.sleep(0.025)
    diffs.append(diff(frame1,frame2))

stream_done=True
print(diffs)
cv2.destroyAllWindows()
camera.close()
```

This one seems to be much faster, and and is able to run the Test without repetitive readouts.

Assuming all you need to do is send messages to a single channel, try adapting this code to your needs. If you need to know how it works, the code is fairly easy to read.
```py
import asyncio, discord, time, threading, readline

loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
bot = discord.Client()
token = 'put a token here'
bot_token = True
message_queue = asyncio.Queue()
channel_id = '250346442500079617'

def bot_thread(loop, bot, bot_token, message_queue, channel_id):
    asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

    @bot.event
    async def on_ready():
        while True:
            data = await message_queue.get()
            event = data[0]
            message = data[1]
            channel_id = data[2]

            try:
                await bot.send_message(bot.get_channel(channel_id), message)
            except:
                pass

            event.set()

    bot.run(token, bot = bot_token)

thread = threading.Thread(target = bot_thread, args = (loop, bot, bot_token, message_queue, channel_id), daemon = True)
thread.start()

def send(channel_id, message):
    event = threading.Event()

    message_queue.put_nowait([event, message, channel_id])

    event.wait()

print('Bot logging in...')

try:
    kek = False
    while not kek:
        time.sleep(0.1)

        if bot._is_ready.is_set(): # wait until the ready event
            while True:
                try:
                    message = input('Message to send > ')
                except:
                    break
                else:
                    send(channel_id, message)

            kek = True
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
```
It was a fun little project for me too.

The code you will most likely want to change is the try/except block at the very bottom and the variables at the top. Everything else can be left as-is.

There's a single function you'll probably be interested in, called `send`. It takes two arguments, `channel_id` and `message`, which sends the (string) `message` to the channel at (string) `channel_id`.

I've refactored a bit the code and pasted the patch down below.
It would make more sense IMHO to specify the target path of the dotfile to compare (for example `~/.vimrc`) instead of the key in the config file, what do you think ?

Beside refactoring the code, my changes allow you to do that for example
`$ ./dotdrop.sh compare --files=~/.vimrc,~/.bashrc` 
which will only compare the two dotfiles *linked* to `~/.vimrc` and `~/.bashrc`.

In case the dotfile referenced does not exist, it will simply report an error but will continue with the rest:
```
$ ./dotdrop.sh compare --files=~/.vimrc,/tmp/xxx
...
[ERR] no dotfile match "/tmp/xxx"
...
```

If that works for you, please apply the patch on your PR and I will merge it.
Thanks

The patch
```
diff --git a/dotdrop/dotdrop.py b/dotdrop/dotdrop.py
index 1d19ae2..ce5d819 100755
--- a/dotdrop/dotdrop.py
+++ b/dotdrop/dotdrop.py
@@ -32,7 +32,7 @@ USAGE = """
 Usage:
   dotdrop.py install [--profile=<profile>] [--cfg=<path>]
     [(-f | --force)] [--nodiff] [--dry]
-  dotdrop.py compare [--profile=<profile>] [--cfg=<path>] [--files=<file key>]
+  dotdrop.py compare [--profile=<profile>] [--cfg=<path>] [--files=<filelist>]
   dotdrop.py list [--cfg=<path>]
   dotdrop.py import [--profile=<profile>] [--cfg=<path>]
     [(-l | --link)] [--dry] <paths>...
@@ -42,6 +42,7 @@ Usage:
 Options:
   --profile=<profiles>    Specify the profile to use [default: %s].
   --cfg=<path>            Path to the config [default: %s/config.yaml].
+  --files=<filelist>      Comma separated list of file to compare.
   --dry                   Dry run.
   --nodiff                Do not diff when installing [default: False].
   -l --link               Import and link [default: False].
@@ -77,7 +78,7 @@ def install(opts, conf):
     return True
 
 
-def compare(opts, conf, tmp, focus=""):
+def compare(opts, conf, tmp, focus=None):
     dotfiles = conf.get_dotfiles(opts['profile'])
     if dotfiles == []:

I think this is a bug:
`Symbol` are only returned if `isliteral` and this is a `Symbol` instance per https://github.com/bastikr/boolean.py/blob/4903f4e7705b576fea3eccacc9c49fe870bcd10b/boolean/boolean.py#L530

And a `NOT` function is set to be a literal itself if its (single) `arg` is itself a literal per https://github.com/bastikr/boolean.py/blob/4903f4e7705b576fea3eccacc9c49fe870bcd10b/boolean/boolean.py#L921

... which would be `True` in the case of `~A` . e.g. This means `NOT` is a literal. But `NOT` is not a `Symbol` ...

And  by this faulty construction, the `NOT` function with a single symbol is therefore a literal and not a `Symbol` instance: therefore its underlying symbol is never returned with `get_symbols()`


@bastikr @Kronuz would you agree this is a bug? I think we should we return the underlying `Symbol` correctly.

The way to perform this conversion is similar to what is done for DS VRs (i.e. I can assign a number and it is automatically converted to a string upon assignment). The initial groundwork actually exists but is commented out [here](https://github.com/darcymason/pydicom/blob/master/pydicom/dataelem.py#L176). 

The tricky thing about PersonName, unlike DS and others, is that the encodings really depend on the dataset whereas with a DS, regardless of which dataset you affiliate the data element with it will be valid.

As a result, if you set the value equal to a unicode string that has different encodings for the phonetic and ideographic components, you have to specify these encodings at the time of the PersonNameUnicode creation if you want it to automatically be converted at assignment and these aren't readily available to the value setter.

I think the better way to handle this would be to just convert it upon saving and if a unicode string is present (rather than an PersonName or PersonNameUnicode instance) attempt to convert it using the character encodings for the dataset and if that fails, then throw an informative error.

Apart from the encoding issue, I need to make sure the use of logging messages is more organised.

When you log messages to CloudWatch Logs, you specify two keys for what log "bucket" your messages go into: log group and log stream. If multiple writers write to the same stream, they encounter a race condition and have to retry submitting the messages, causing an unnecessary performance issue and, in case of too many writers, a crash.

To solve this problem, we have to assign a unique name to each writer. As pointed out above, Lambda uses what looks like a uuid at the end of its log stream name. I'd like at least the prefix of the name to be deterministic, if not the whole name, e.g. watchtower/logger-name/instance-id/process-id.

Select is not required. Same can be done with `timeout=None` all blocking operations will wait until some data is available in the buffer.

Do you have PyICU installed?  I have found that python's built-in `locale` library (which does the work of understanding local-dependent sorting) does not work properly on some systems.  If you have PyICU installed, `natsort` will use that under the hood, and it gives better results.  Can you try that?

```python
kf = natsort.natsort_keygen( alg = (natsort.ns.LOCALE | natsort.ns.TYPESAFE ) )
ll = ['0','Á','2',b'Z']
sorted(map(str, ll),key=kf)
```

I'm a little bit confused about where `b'*\x05 \x01E\x88\x01\x06\x00'` is coming from.  Are you calling `".encode()"` on your list elements before sorting?

FYI: natsort returns a tuple for all inputs because it has to split the numbers from the strings, but keep them logically grouped together.  An empty string is placed before numbers to avoid unorderable types errors (please see issue #7). Sorting tuples is well defined. A key can return anything as long as it is orderable, which does not seem to be the case if `bytes` and `str` are mixed.

@job, @tking, thank you for your comments on this topic.

Since the beginning I was torn about whether to add RPKI light to the features of this tool. Personally I would not use it in a route server; I believe that if something needs to be validated, it must be done within the boundaries of my trust domain, that only includes components I manage directly, so - as a client - I would not appreciate such a feature.

Your comments led me to reconsider all the messages appeared on sidrops and grow and finally I decided to keep this feature somehow but also to "discourage" its use.

Currently, RPKI handling is limited to a global flag in the general configuration file, `reject_invalid`, that is True by default.
This means that
- routes received by the route server from its clients are validated then
- custom locally-significant communities, if provided within the configuration, are attached to the routes depending on their validity state;
- if the `reject_invalid` flag is True, INVALID ones are dropped;
- remaining routes (including INVALID ones if `reject_invalid` is False) are announced to the clients.

Now I plan to add more granularity to the use of RPKI
- by allowing client-specific level configuration of the `reject_invalid` flag
- by introducing an additional client-specific boolean flag: `announce_invalid`, False by default;
- by lowering local-pref of INVALIDs.

The mix of the two flags and of communities à la RPKI-light allows to have the route server able to receive INVALID routes and to announce them only to clients that have been configured with `announce_invalid: True` and which are supposedly ready to correctly interpret and handle them.

So:
- default values would have the route server to discard INVALIDs received by clients;
- `reject_invalid: False` and `announce_invalid` would lead the route server to propagate INVALIDs to those clients for which the latter flag has been set to True.
In the second scenario, in the case where no `roa_invalid` communities (those used to mark INVALID routes) were provided within the configuration, an error would be logged and the configuration building process stopped, to prevent blind announcement of INVALID routes to clients that, in that case, would not be able to identify them.

Fine configuration granularity and an opt-in mechanism to receive INVALIDs seem to be a quite commonly accepted outcome among people who participated in the conversation on grow too.

The bottom line is that with ARouteServer I would like to provide a tool in the hands of IXP staff, possibly something that is easy to use and that offers *by default* feature-rich configurations with low effort. While I am determined to distribute a default configuration that is as secure as possible for the whole Internet, I can't even imagine to know all the deployment scenarios that IXPs have to cover, so I prefer to leave them freedom of choice.

I'll do my best to follow this draft on sidrops and grow and to evaluate further actions on the basis of its evolution.

Thanks again for your time on this.

So currently we create three prior collections:

```
shared_lens_light_priors = PriorCollection(UniformPrior('centre_x'), UniformPrior('centre_y'))
lens_light_sersic_1_priors = PriorCollections(...)
lens_light_sersic_2_priors = PriorCollections(...)
```

In this formalism we need a unit hypercube vector corresponding to each individual collection. That is converted into a dictionary of arguments by the collection:

```
sersic_1_arguments = lens_light_sersic_1_priors.arguments_for_vector(vector1)
sersic_2_arguments = lens_light_sersic_2_priors.arguments_for_vector(vector2)
shared_lens_arguments = shared_lens_light_priors.arguments_for_vector(vector3)
```

The individual sersic arguments are updated using the shared arguments:

```
sersic_1_arguments.update(shared_lens_arguments)
sersic_2_arguments.update(shared_lens_arguments)
```

And finally the sersic profiles are instantiated using the combined arguments:

```
sersic_1 = EllipticalSersic(**sersic_1_arguments)
sersic_2 = EllipticalSersic(**sersic_2_arguments)
```

This has some aspect of manually mapping unit vectors to classes. We may want to encode the associated classes and their structure into the PriorCollection some how.

`from pdfminer.pdfinterp import PDFResourceManager, PDFPageInterpreter
from pdfminer.converter import TextConverter
from pdfminer.layout import LAParams
from pdfminer.pdfpage import PDFPage
from cStringIO import StringIO`

`def convert_pdf_to_txt(self, path):
        rsrcmgr = PDFResourceManager()
        retstr = StringIO()
        codec = 'utf-8'
        laparams = LAParams()
        device = TextConverter(rsrcmgr, retstr, codec=codec, laparams=laparams)
        fp = file(path, 'rb')
        interpreter = PDFPageInterpreter(rsrcmgr, device)
        password = ""
        maxpages = 0
        caching = True
        pagenos = set()

        for page in PDFPage.get_pages(fp, pagenos, maxpages=maxpages, password=password, caching=caching,
                                      check_extractable=True):
            interpreter.process_page(page)

        text = retstr.getvalue()

        fp.close()
        device.close()
        retstr.close()
        return text`

`def extract_pdfminer(self, filename):
        """Extract text from pdfs using pdfminer."""
        stdout = self.convert_pdf_to_txt(filename)
        return stdout`

leave the choice of what to do with super args up to the user -- if there's
cooperative multiple inheritance, there may be fields that are not known
from looking at the current class and its MRO. And these need to be dealt
with using **kwds. But not every user will want this -- if they don't care
about cooperative MI, they're welcome to just call the immediate super
class's method with the exact arguments it takes.

You can overrride the format_username function with your own
implementation, and do what you need. That's what the
LDAP_AUTH_FORMAT_USERNAME setting is for.

I'm confused about why this broke for you in the latest release, though.
The logic for authentication is:

1. LDAP bind using the supplied username and password. Abort if fail.
2. If LDAP_AUTH_CONNECTION_USERNAME is set, rebind with that.
3. Query the ldap database for the user details. If not found, abort.

Step 2 is what's changed in the new release.

However, the whole point of LDAP_AUTH_CONNECTION_USERNAME is that it should
specify an account for querying the ldap database. It's also used by the
ldap_sync_users management command. It should be able to access all users
in the system.

Hi @PhilipGarnero,

I don't think you understand correctly the flow that we talk. I will try to explain you my use case.

Since the new version of the Android SDK, Google prefers the `Authorization Code flow` over the `Implicit flow`. So when on my android device I click on the button `Continue with Google`, Google returns me a one time authorization code and not an access token. The authorization code needs to be exchanged for an access token on the server to respect the new security standard.

My suggestion is to add a new grant-type to the convert token to support this feature.
```
{
    "grant_type": "authorization-code",
    "backend": "<backend>",
    "code": "<social_authorization_code>"
}
```

This will call google and convert the authorization code to an access token. 
- If the user doesn't exist in the database, we need to create it. After that, we have to return the token of the Django Application
- If the user exists in the database, we only need to return the token of the Django Application

If you want to test this flow with Facebook, here is a screenshot of my application configuration:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4728325/22217557/6ff970c2-e171-11e6-83b8-af0cfb539ae0.png)

If you want more information on facebook, here is a video that explains the authorization code flow: https://www.facebook.com/FacebookforDevelopers/videos/10152795636318553/
If you want more information on google: https://developers.google.com/actions/develop/identity/oauth2-code-flow

If you have any questions or if we can help you implement this feature, let me know.

Thanks

Hi @KennethNielsen,

I think it is great idea to have a tool for debugging events, and it can also be something that users can use to give us bug reports. I am in favour of anything that makes life easier!  But I am not 100% sure at the moment that I understand what problem the code in events.py is trying to solve. To my mind, all it is doing is logging information to a file, and allowing an event stream to be parsed, and that is something that should be done by the logging module, and by the developer tool itself. I may be misunderstanding what you are doing though.

> Making it easy to add something to an issue that could be used to reproduce the bug exactly

Yes.

> Making it easy for developers to replay those this and recreate the bug

Yes please!


> Making 100% sure, that what was passed into the parse function on the machine that created the error, is the same thing that is passed into the parse function of a developer machine

OK. But see my comments on unicode below.

> Your solution has a little more boiler plate, but not much, so ok for 1. 

It can be made shorter if necessary. I did it this way for clarity.


> If we are to not add any new code to events.py at all, then 2 cannot be 
> achieved. The developer will have to craft the replay code himself.

I don't understand this bit. I'm sorry. If the logging module has logged the event as it has come in, and before it is processed by `parse_event_xml`, why can the developer tool not just do the 'replay' itself, by reading the log, extracting the event, and passing it through `parse_event_xml` itself, and then printing out the value of the evented variables? In other words, just put something like your `replay_event_stream` into the developer tool, rather than in `events.py`.  That way, all the debugging code is kept where I think it really belongs - in the developer tool.


> 
> The last thing is one of the more annoying and this is one of string encoding and raw vs. unicode input to logging. From a quick scan of the documentation, both for 2 and 3 it says that "msg is the format string" which I guess means that it takes python 2 str on python 2 and python 3 str on python 3. The parse function is passed bytes, and we really need to make sure those are preserved exactly. So supposed we decode and pass python 3 logging a unicode string, the it will have to be re-encoded before written to a file and with what character set, system default? 

Again, I don't understand I'm afraid.   At the moment, the method `do_NOTIFY` logs the precise bytes received (the line which says `log.debug("Event content: %s", content)`). So the bytes received *are* preserved, in the log. If the developer tool sets up logging to log to a file, it can specify the encoding to use when the bytes are written to a file.

>This will cause problems when bug reported and developer are on different platforms. One possibly solution could be to add an extra call to debug, where the content was base64 encoded, or something like that, that is dead certain to have the same representation regardless of character encoding of the file.
> 

This is not necessary - just specify the encoding of the file to use when the logging file handler is created.

> This can surely be fixed by thoroughly reading the documentation and making some tests, but it was just so straight forward to simply write the bytes the parse function.
> 
> In any case, if we do decide to go purely with logging output, then I see to reason to filter in advance. The view tool can easily do that afterwards, and so could the replay code, whether it lives in events.py or in the tool.
>

We are also experiencing this issue now. We rely need a new urllib to fix multiple bugs. Boto3 is currently not usable for our company.

`resume_token` is currently only set once `MaxItems` has been reached:

```python
import boto3


ddb = boto3.client('dynamodb')

# Setup an expected response
stubber = Stubber(ddb)
last_evaluated_key = {"data": {"S": "value"}}
service_response = {
    "ScannedCount": 1,
    "Count": 1,
    "Items": [last_evaluated_key],
    "LastEvaluatedKey": last_evaluated_key
}
stubber.add_response('scan', service_response)

# Scan for a single item, using MaxItems to make sure resume_token is set
paginator = ddb.get_paginator('scan')
pages = paginator.paginate(TableName='testing-table', PaginationConfig={
    'MaxItems': 1, 'PageSize': 1})

with stubber:
    for page in pages:
        pass

# Print paging arguments
print(page.get('LastEvaluatedKey'))
print(pages.resume_token)
```

For now your only option in that case is the `LastEvaluatedKey` which is unfortunate.

We could set it every time, but I'm not comfortable doing that because [the setter](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/paginate.py#L71) would negatively impact performance (possibly noticeably, especially in the dynamodb case).

We could also potentially set it only in error cases, so this would work:

```python
import boto3
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError


ddb = boto3.client('dynamodb')

ddb = sess.create_client('dynamodb')

# Setup an expected response
stubber = Stubber(ddb)
last_evaluated_key = {"data": {"S": "value"}}
service_response = {
    "ScannedCount": 1,
    "Count": 1,
    "Items": [last_evaluated_key],
    "LastEvaluatedKey": last_evaluated_key
}
stubber.add_response('scan', service_response)

# Setup an error
stubber.add_client_error('scan')

# Scan for two items, where the second call will throw an exception
paginator = ddb.get_paginator('scan')
pages = paginator.paginate(TableName='testing-table', PaginationConfig={
    'MaxItems': 2, 'PageSize': 1})

with stubber:
    try:
        list(pages)
    except ClientError:
        print(pages.resume_token)
```

I'm leaning towards that second option. There is still the possibility that you will not be able to use that `resume_token` as the tokens for some services do expire. What do you think?

In this case, it's because of these lines in `check-repos`:
```shell
docker images | grep $appname | awk '{ print $3 }' | xargs docker rmi -f
docker images --filter "dangling=true" | awk '{ print $3 }' | grep -v IMAGE | xargs docker rmi -f
```
`docker images` may return nothing, and `docker rmi` returns 123 if not given any further arguments. We can fix this by using [`xargs -r`](https://www.gnu.org/software/findutils/manual/html_node/find_html/xargs-options.html), so that the target command is never run unless there are arguments for it. `xargs` default is to run once regardless.

@krancour Something that is not really discussed in the k8s HA docs that should be discussed is ETCD continued availability over cluster churn.  I'm going to talk about this specifically in an AWS environment since that's how we are running our cluster.

When a node running ETCD is terminated it is not removed from the nodes list.  If you are using the discovery URL for ETCD you have to tell it the initial cluster size.  If you don't specify the initial cluster size it assumes it is 3.  If the initial cluster size is 3 and you launch 5 nodes that connect through the ETCD discovery URL the default behavior is to have the first 3 nodes that connect become the nodes that control the quorum and the other 2 nodes to be proxy nodes.  If a proxy node dies and AWS fires off an autoscaling event and brings up a new node it does not cause a problem, but if a master node dies it will stay a part of the cluster and the new node that comes up will become a proxy node as this is now the 6th node in the cluster.  After the node comes up you will be left with 2 master nodes and if a second master node dies you will then have a broken cluster.

TLDR;
* ETCD Discovery URL expects a static cluster size
* If a node is terminated it must be removed from the ETCD Discovery URL cluster listing or any additional nodes will be proxies
* Something must remove the terminated node prior to the autoscaling event of a new server

Some light reading on the proxy-fallback mechanism https://github.com/coreos/etcd/issues/1457
Some light reading on Adding/Removing Nodes from ETCD https://github.com/coreos/etcd/blob/master/Documentation/runtime-configuration.md#replace-a-failed-machine

Thanks for the detailed problem description!

The running time of the algorithm is linear in the number of _nonzero_ item
and user features: if the average number of nonzero features per intem
increases from 1 (no item features) to 10, you can expect a (somewhat less
than) tenfold increase in training time. This in not because of an
inefficient implementation: it's just that more vector multiplications are
now required.

It looks like your observations are consistent with this expectation, so
all looks normal. To reduce training time I would recommend using
multithreading. You will need a Linux machine for this, because clang does
not support openmp.

About this PR:
The initial motivation is that currently optimizer is not modifying the input model at all so const reference is more appropriate than unique_ptr. 
Changing the optimizer to do in-place update involves two parts:
1. When converting the input ModelProto to a Graph, let the Graph create some internal pointers pointing to the underlying data (e.g. initializers) in the ModelProto. I think this largely deviates from the current design of the Graph class, and will need to rewrite a pretty large portion of the current IR classes.
2. Write the output ModelProto directly to the input ModelProto, instead of creating a new ModelProto as return value like right now. This part can be done without 1, the change should be small. And it will keep the possibility to do 1 in the future without changing the api interface.

@ezyang About the criticism to non-const reference:
I know there is this rule in Google's C++ style guide and never see it anywhere else. I don't agree with it and in fact the [C++ Core Guidelines](https://github.com/isocpp/CppCoreGuidelines/blob/master/CppCoreGuidelines.md) explicitly lists non-const reference as valid usage for in-out parameters.
Also I'm a little bit surprised by you saying this because I see there are many non-const references function parameters in PyTorch :-)

@linkerzhang DATA_BATCH, DATA_CHANNEL, DATA_FEATURE are not numbers. In fact they are not quantities; they are descriptions. Think of them as strings (as they really are), then we shouldn't try to multiply them together. In addition, I think we should be even more explicit here because multiplication is commutative. Thus:
```
DATA_BATCH * DATA_CHANNEL * DATA_FEATURE * DATA_FEATURE = 
DATA_BATCH * DATA_FEATURE * DATA_FEATURE * DATA_CHANNEL
```

Instead we should probably simply say, i.e. for conv, "Convolution operation expects input tensor X to be in the format of (Batch, Channel, Feature [, Feature[, Feature]])." Let me know what you think and I can move forward with clarifying op descriptions.

I think this should be reopened because as is, `pipenv install` creates environments that immediately fail `check`. For example:

`Pipfile`:

```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
testpackage = "*"

[requires]
python_full_version = "3.5.3"
implementation_name = "pypy"
implementation_version = "5.10.1"
```

`pipenv install` will create the venv with CPython (probably, unless you _only_ have pypy installed), but `pipenv check` will then immediately fail:

```
$ pipenv check
Checking PEP 508 requirements…
Specifier implementation_name does not match pypy (cpython).
Specifier implementation_version does not match 5.10.1 (3.5.3).
Failed!
```

`Pipfile.lock`:

<details>

```json
{
    "_meta": {
        "hash": {
            "sha256": "c637dfe39ab17bf44ba426526e66497c8e9d4663eafa9214c7e5edcb13b0d7e2"
        },
        "host-environment-markers": {
            "implementation_name": "cpython",
            "implementation_version": "3.5.3",
            "os_name": "posix",
            "platform_machine": "x86_64",
            "platform_python_implementation": "CPython",
            "platform_release": "17.4.0",
            "platform_system": "Darwin",
            "platform_version": "Darwin Kernel Version 17.4.0: Sun Dec 17 09:19:54 PST 2017; root:xnu-4570.41.2~1/RELEASE_X86_64",
            "python_full_version": "3.5.3",
            "python_version": "3.5",
            "sys_platform": "darwin"
        },
        "pipfile-spec": 6,
        "requires": {
            "implementation_name": "pypy",
            "implementation_version": "5.10.1",
            "python_full_version": "3.5.3"
        },
        "sources": [
            {
                "name": "pypi",
                "url": "https://pypi.python.org/simple",
                "verify_ssl": true
            }
        ]
    },
    "default": {
        "testpackage": {
            "hashes": [
                "sha256:fd0076de3a2c8c695826e466cbb0f9febc6ea8474302ec3e673dbe46cd278b6a"
            ],
            "version": "==2.27"
        }
    },
    "develop": {}
}
```

</details>

The expected behavior would be that anything in the `requires` section of the `Pipfile` should be considered for `install` such that pipenv doesn't end up creating environments that fail the `check` process with no changes. For this example, that would mean looking at the python implementation type when finding a matching python executable. I believe only `python_full_version` and `python_version` are currently used during `install`.

Of course, not all PEP508 markers will be useful as a `requires` directive, but if they are given in the `Pipfile` and don't match, an error should be given if the criteria are not able to be satisfied.

This feature would be very useful for anyone that requires non CPython runtimes.

@techalchemy I don't think this would need to use pyenv shims. I think when selecting the python runtime, pipenv will just have to additionally examine `implementation_version` and `implementation_name`. That's something I might try out soon when I get the chance. Limitations on that would be that any runtime with `python_version` less than 3.3 will report those markers as `cpython` and `0`. In that case the only recourse is to specify the full path to the executable.

I'm confused by this too, but I understand things a bit differently:

**Old world:**

- `setup.py` contains abstract dependencies
    - for libraries, these dependencies affect other packages that use the library
    - for applications, this is mostly used by developers working on new versions
- `requirements.txt` contains concrete dependencies
    - not used for libraries
    - used for repeatable deployment of applications

**New world:**

- `setup.py` contains abstract dependencies for libraries (so that they can be stored in wheels and uploaded to PyPI)
- `Pipfile` contains abstract dependencies for applications (used by developers working on new versions)
- `Pipfile.lock` contains concrete dependencies

I wish abstract dependencies could be in the same place, in the same (non-Turing-complete) format for both libraries and applications.

Hi @milan-janecek the help text is correct. The generic updates look something like `<verb> <path> <value>` (i.e. set myList[0] "foo"). So internally the paths are converted from camelCase to python_case.  However, the value portion is always just treated as is, which is why, when you set a whole JSON object, they need to already be in python_case.

That's the explanation, but we can definitely look into applying that same conversion to the keys of a parsed JSON object.

**Answer:**
@asasine regarding the main issue you have raised, after speaking to some members of the team, it appears that -o tsv supports the functionality you want. it outputs a raw unquoted string.

**Other comments:**
Concerning the other issue / bug you raised, apparently it is not a bug per se but a quirk of the fact there isn't a clear way to represent an object as a tsv. As a result we do not currently handle including json object values in the resulting tsv file.

So for example if you run:

`az group list --query "[].{n:name, l:location}" -o tsv`

You will get a list of your resource groups as a tsv file with names in the first column and locations in the second column.

However if you run:

`az group list --query "[].{n:name, l:location, p: properties}" -o tsv`

Your output will look exactly the same as before. The properties field, which is a json object, will not be included in the tsv output. 

The team might eventually deal with this issue by updating `-o tsv` to support json object values by simply adding a new column with a flattened version of the object as a value, but for now it looks like we will leave it as it is.

I will be closing this issue, I hope this helps.

VS110COMNTOOLS=C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 11.0\Common7\Tools\
VS120COMNTOOLS=C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 12.0\Common7\Tools\
VS140COMNTOOLS=C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 14.0\Common7\Tools\

You can set them to your VS installation path manually.  However, the setuptools we use should be able to autodetect your compiler, so rather first verify you are using the latest version:
`pip install --upgrade setuptools`

https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=44266

I've opened https://github.com/esnme/ultrajson/issues/264 to see what upstream thinks about disallowing `bytes`, but I don't really expect that to happen because ujson's approach seems to generally be "Serialize it all and let Douglas Crockford sort 'em out". For example:

```
>>> ujson.dumps({"hi": set()})
'{"hi":[]}'
>>> ujson.dumps({"hi": object()})
'{"hi":{}}'
>>> class MyFoo:
...   def __init__(self, a, b):
...     self.a = a
...     self.b = b
... 
>>> ujson.dumps(MyFoo(1, 'a'))
'{"a":1,"b":"a"}'
```

```
>>> json.dumps({"hi": set()})

    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: set() is not JSON serializable
>>> json.dumps({"hi": object()})

    return _default_encoder.encode(obj)

    return _iterencode(o, 0)

    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")

I agree with @asmeurer. A leading underscore is simply a convention that the function, method, or var is private, an implementation detail, not part of the public API of the module or class. It says absolutely nothing about the symbol not being used. Only the magic `_` symbol is special in that regard. And only because it is a widely used convention at companies like Google:

```
def my_func(param_a, param_b):
    _ = param_b  # this param is not used
```

The normal recommendation for dealing with Bitlinks is to store, at the very least, the target URL and the Bitlink itself in a data store of some kind. That way you avoid re-shortening the same link, which wastes your rate limit, and you have a record of what you've done. When you need a Bitlink for a URL, look first in your data store. If you find the record, use that information. If you do not find a record, shorten the URL via the API, then store the result in your data store.

We have some valuable debugging results. It seems that H2O doesn't support multihoming, which is quite a typical thing, as it is not supported by Hadoop in general.

Context: we have our Cloudera Hadoop cluster deployed on specialized hardware, called Big Data Aplliance (BDA), an Oracle product. Multihoming is used in Big Data Appliance, as cluster nodes communicate with each other via InfiniBand using their internal network, using INTERNAL IP addresses and they communicate with the rest of P&G intranet using EXTERNAL IP addresses.

CDH (and Hadoop in general) doesn’t support multihoming (cluster nodes belonging to multiple networks). Multihoming is supported for some appliances (BDA being one of them), but our edge nodes are not within the BDA, which is a non-standard setup. So when you add non-BDA nodes you are out of the supported/recommended configuration from both the Oracle side and Cloudera side. It is not sub-optimal setup. It is just that Hadoop and related technologies (unfortunately) have not really been designed with multi-homed networking in mind.

This causes connectivity issues, as (according to a Cloudera expert):

> Historically we have had issues running pyspark from non-BDA nodes because of similar issues. We have also had issues running spark shell that we have worked around by specifying IP addresses instead of hostnames.

This hypothesis was confirmed by running Sparkling Water directly on one of the cluster nodes:
We tried to run Sparkling Water on a BDA node and it seems to work fine. We used sparkling-water-1.5.6 and steps from http://h2o-release.s3.amazonaws.com/sparkling-water/rel-1.5/6/index.html (the RUN ON HADOOP tab). An example command like: 
`$ spark-submit --class water.SparklingWaterDriver --master yarn-client --num-executors 8 --driver-memory 8g --executor-memory 4g --executor-cores 1 assembly/build/libs/*.jar `
worked fine.

*Proposer:* Philip Bauer[[BR]]
*Seconder:* Johannes Raggam[[BR]]


## Abstract

Make Collection, Event, Document, News Items and Link folderish by default by inheriting from plone.dexterity.content.Container. Image and File stay itemish unless we find a use-case for those.


## Motivation

This PLIP would:

- Remove a inconsistency between the default-types and types that are generated ttw (they are folderish).
- Allow developers and users to easily make the types folderish by changing the values for filter_content_types and/or allowed_content_types
- This way editors can create site-structures without using the default-page feature.
- Prepare for a possible removal of the default_page-feature


## Proposal & Implementation

We would simply change the base-class of said types to Container. For new sites no other changes are necessary.

Objects in existing sites need to be updated. That upgrade-step runs when upgrading from Plone 5.0a3 to the next version and is also available as a upgrade-step in plone.app.contenttypes (e.g. if you use p.a.c in Plone 4).

From a usability-perspective nothing changes for normal users. Since the FTI's are configured like this: <property name="allowed_content_types"/> you cannot add any types. Only if this setting is changed content can be added to these types.


## Deliverables

- Change the base-class (DONE)
- Upgrade-Step to change existing objects (DONE)
- Don't show the menu-item to add content to a folderish default_page if no content can be added to it (DONE)
- Show folderish types in @@select_default_page (DONE)
- Tests (DONE)
- Documentation (TODO)
- Fix test-failures in other packages (TODO)


## Risks

- Perfomance implications: All tests (including robot-tests) ran in same time.
- Memory-footprint implications: We need to find out if the switch significanlty increases the memory-use of types.
- UI-Problems:
  - Adding content to folderish default_pages. This generally works since there is the link "Add item to default page" but it's use could be irritating. By default the link would never show up with the changes in this PLIP since you cannot add content to these types unless you add some types to allowed_content_types for a FTI. If this is the case the type would no longer be selectable as a default_page. For now you can only override this by changing default_page_types in site_properties. This only makes sense in cases where you don't create a structure using folderish documents but if you store images and files inside the document.
  - There is also a js-problem with the link "Add item to default page" when @@folder-factories is opened in a popup and you select a type to be added you are redirected to the container instead of to the ++add++ view.
- The new folder_contents shows the add-menu for folderish types even if allowed_content_types is empty.

## Patricipants

Philip Bauer <pbauer>


## Progress
Work in plone.app.contenttypes is done in the branch See https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contenttypes/pull/196

* Change to folderish: DONE
* Upgrade-step: DONE
* Show folderish types in @@select_default_page. The folderish types weere skipped, now they are only skipped if if users can add content. DONE in https://github.com/plone/plone.app.content/commit/f745a13e2d2dd89e2dfcf51cb5d00acc05b29c80. I already commited this since it is also a problem for custom folderish types (like those that are generated ttw).
* Don't show the menu-item to add content to a folderish default_page if no content can be added to it. DONE in https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contentmenu/pull/6. I already did this since it is also a problem for custom folderish types (like those that are generated ttw).
* Test for the upgrade-step: DONE
* Test for folderish types: DONE

## Alternatives

1. A optional folderish profile to allow users and developers to have a supported way to get folderish default-types is in the pull-request https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contenttypes/pull/195.

2. collective.folderishtypes provides folderish types (for AT and DX) as an addon: https://github.com/collective/collective.folderishtypes

i think rdflib is right in this case...

according to the [URI RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#appendix-A) and the [IRI RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3987#section-2.2) no part of a URI/IRI can contain contain a `|` (ASCII hex 7C).

As far as i can see this also holds true for the others in
```
_invalid_uri_chars = '<>" {}|\\^\`'
```
(just double checked them and those seem to be all of the printable ASCII chars which are invalid)

It's possible (and likely) though, that we don't catch all/any ASCII control chars with the mentioned check... maybe that should be added / we should just rely on underlying / third party libs to parse the URIs...

I ran this test case ~5k times for different scenarios on a Linux GPU machine (EC2 p2.8x large instance). Currently the reasons for test failure are -

2. The bigger bug is that the test intermittently hangs while performing the ``check_numeric_gradient``. ``check_symbolic_backward`` and ``check_symbolic_forward`` works correctly and consistently.

It seems that the 'REPLACE INTO' syntax is supported for [MySQL](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/replace.html) and [SQLite](http://www.sqlitetutorial.net/sqlite-replace-statement/), but looks slightly different for other engines.

I'm a bit unhappy about introducing the `replace=` parameter just for `mysql_hook.insert_rows`, as this seems to be a useful feature for all engines, and in doing it just for one engine, we would also copy over a lot of generic code from `dbapi_hook` to the mysql-specific implementation (with the possibly happening as for other implementations as well as soon we start supporting that feature for these).

After some deeper look into the code, one way to solve that would be splitting out the sql string generation process into two separate methods (one for insert and one for replace, with the latter raising `NotImplementedError` in `dbapi_hook`), and overiding those in the engine specific classes. From looking at the test BitFieldTest.test_update,  the issue here is that mysql and postgres interpret the following statement differently:

```sql
UPDATE `tests_bitfieldtestmodel` SET `another_name` = `tests_bitfieldtestmodel`.`another_name` | -2 WHERE `tests_bitfieldtestmodel`.`id` = 8 
```

In postgres the result is 14 (like expected), but in mysql, the resulting value is 9223372036854775807.  This is due to an out-of-range error that just sets the column to the max int.

Still looking into a fix, but I dont see any way this could have worked in the past either.

This is expected. .Net Framework 4.6.1 behaves as if it supported .Net Standard 2.0 and it does support the vast majority of it, but not everything. Specifically, it does not support APIs that were added in .Net Standard 1.5 and 1.6.

For more details, see [.NET Framework 4.6.1 supporting .NET Standard 2.0](https://github.com/dotnet/standard/tree/master/docs/netstandard-20#net-framework-461-supporting-net-standard-20).

As for your specific case, Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyModel 2.0.4 has versions for .Net Framework 4.5.1, .Net Standard 1.3 and .Net Standard 1.6. How exactly are you distributing your application/library? Maybe there's a way to use the .Net Framework 4.5.1 version of Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyModel from your .Net Framework 4.6.2 host?

Regarding part 3: The good thing about `__signature__` is that it's essentially invisible but it's part of the standard `inspect` module. Starting with Python 3.4, `inspect.getargspec` and `inspect.getfullargspec` internally first check for the existence of `__signature__` and fall back to older introspection otherwise (see https://bugs.python.org/issue17481).

Both PyCharm and Sphinx use `getargspec` to inspect regular functions and docstring parsing for binaries. A change would be needed on their end, but only a small one to essentially try `getargspec` on binaries as well (and fall back to docstrings on exception). This was also the idea for Python builtins as per the discussion in the issue linked above. It doesn't seem to be very widely adopted (probably because it's Python 3.4+ only), but the infrastructure is in place. There might be some other things to work out, which is why this would be best as a separate PR. I mainly wanted to ask if this would be interesting.

A `static_assert` isn't possible because it all depends on the return value policy. It would be possible to `pybind11_fail` at runtime, but that would require keeping the new conditions and also adding an `else` with the failure -- even more code. At that point, might as well just support it.

In C++, even if a type is usually held by a `std::shared_ptr` it's not uncommon to still have just a simple `const&` accessor because we know in C++ that the reference never implies ownership. This is in contrast to a raw pointer, where I would definitely consider it problematic in conjunction with `std::shared_ptr`. Taking a non-owning reference seems like a perfectly valid use case that should not segfault.

Thanks for the deep review! To answer your points.


1. The API. I don't especially care about what it looks like. At the high-level, the only things we need are:

- a public API to access current trace_id/span_id/sampling_priority.
- a public API to create a local trace from remote/propagated trace_id/span_id/sampling_priority.

And when I say a public API, it can be any combination of public methods, as long as it is clear and documented.


2. The mix of 2 samplers is a way to combine the two approaches in a compatible way.

- `sampler` is the older client-side sampling, simply dropping data according to a rate. When dropped, it doesn't even reach the Agent, so no stats. But also no performance footprint. In practice, not used that much. Disabled by default.
- `distributed_sampler` is the one deciding in advance if the trace should be dropped by the Agent, and it propagates this decision.
The goal: to have it enabled by default, applying a sampling rate per service, provided by the Agent.

I guess in the future we will be able to merge these 2 together, but that was too dangerous to consider so early.

In my use case, you might as well think of the topic as "user activity updates". The number of users is much greater than the number of partitions. User updates are hashed into partitions by unique user id. Each consumer in the group is responsible for aggregating user updates into an up-to-date user data structure per user. The data structure is large and the update heuristic moderately complex, precluding a database read before each update or an atomic in-database stored procedure style update.

Because of the hashed partitions, any given consumer knows that it is the sole updater for any given user whose messages it consumes. The consumer just needs a mechanism to know when to flush/finalize data for a given user if a rebalance occurs and it is no longer the consumer of the partition containing a given user. So... a rebalance callback seems like the natural way to support the use case - though if you'd come at it from a different angle, I would be interested to hear your thoughts.

The problem I am having is with new consumer groups and ```auto_offset_reset=-1```.  In this case, there are no offsets stored, but according to https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/A+Guide+To+The+Kafka+Protocol#AGuideToTheKafkaProtocol-OffsetFetchRequest, this is not an error.

> Note that if there is no offset associated with a topic-partition under that consumer group the broker does not set an error code (since it is not really an error), but returns empty metadata and sets the offset field to -1.

This makes ```_handle_success``` for ```fetch_offsets``` be called, which then calls ```owned_partition.set_offset(pres.offset)```.  In this case, Kafka has returned -1, so ```pres.offset``` is -1.  That is then passed to ``set_offset```, and subsequently incremented to 0 by:

```
self.next_offset = last_offset_consumed + 1
```
On L663 of simpleconsumer.py.

The consumer then proceeds to make a fetch_request at offset 0, which causes the consumer to start consuming from the beginning of the stream.

It feels a little weird to me to have `get_field` be a method of the `Bolt` class instead of just having it so we could access the values by name directly, since [that's how it works in Java](https://nathanmarz.github.io/storm/doc/backtype/storm/tuple/Tuple.html) (see all of the `...ByField` methods).  What about if we did something like this where we modified the `Tuple` class we have to have  a `get_field` method and a `_fields` dictionary like this:

```python
class Tuple(namedtuple):
     def get_field(self, field_name):
          return self.values[self._fields[field_name]]
```

and we modified `Component.read_tuple` to be something like this:

```python
class Component(object):
   ...
   def read_tuple(self):
        cmd = self.read_command()
        tup = Tuple(cmd['id'], cmd['comp'], cmd['stream'], cmd['task'],
                    cmd['tuple'])
        # Set the mapping from field names to indices
        tup._fields = self._field_dict[tup.stream]

You should only get this error message if a file in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` has been deleted. In general, `/etc/letsencrypt` is very sensitive to changes as a number of assumptions are made in the code about the layout of the directory.

What's going on here is Certbot is picking the lineage name (the name used for the directories in `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` and `/etc/letsencrypt/live` and the file in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal`) based on the files found in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal`. It finds an available file name and when it goes to create the `/etc/letsencrypt/archive` directory, the name is already taken which Certbot does not expect.

Instead, we can check the name is available in all three places before deciding on a name. We'd need to be careful here to avoid race conditions. We probably either want to create all three files/directories before choosing the name (and deleting the files/directories if it's determined to be "taken") or implement locks as described in #993. Maybe both to protect against other programs that might not respect our locks.

Those both sound like useful features. However, I don't think they solve the issue of automated deployments (e.g., via SaltStack or similar) that I described.

In the simpler scenario, where you tell the LE client to modify your nginx config, automated deploys still break.  During the next deploy to the same server, the deploy process will overwrite the (unrecognized, not-known-good) modified config with a deployment-managed config.

In the other scenario, where you tell the LE client to modify-and-revert your nginx config, you still have a chicken-and-egg problem. Nginx needs to be running, which means you need to have deployed a config. But ping-ponging between configs on every push is bad news, so the config already needs to reference the SSL cert file locations. But those files don't exist yet because the LE client hasn't run. But to generate those files nginx needs to be running....

@oxtopus, I agree that as a proof of concept, this exercise is a success, especially if we wish to produce a separate one-off nupic just for the Windows Unicorn app. 

However, if we wish to modify the existing public nupic code base, I believe that we would need some additional intervention to avoid polluting the code with unnecessary overhead and complexity. For example:
* changes like `dict(list...` add unnecessary overhead in both python 3.x and 2.x.
* changes like `len(list(kwds.keys())) >= 1` make the code work on python 3.x, while adding unnecessary overhead on both 3.x and 2.x and making the code less clean/more complicated
* changes like `for i in xrange(len(patternNZHistoryProto))` -> `for i in range(len(patternNZHistoryProto))` preserve the same level of performance in 3.x, but add unnecessary overhead in 2.x
* It might be a good opportunity to fix up the other case where legacy code was using the wrong primitive or pattern, since the 2to3 tool's code changes do such a good job of bringing those faults to our attention.

The minimum requirement is the username (or at least, the one defined in `USERNAME_FIELD`), not the user_id (which defaults to `user.pk` in drf-jwt code) :

> Django expects your custom User model to meet some minimum requirements. 
> Your model must have a single unique field that can be used for identification purposes. This can be a username, an email address, or any other unique attribute. 
> Your model must provide a way to address the user in a “short” and “long” form. The most common interpretation of this would be to use the user’s given name as the “short” identifier, and the user’s full name as the “long” identifier. However, there are no constraints on what these two methods return - if you want, they can return exactly the same value.

(from [django docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/auth/customizing/#specifying-a-custom-user-model))

The root cause of what you're seeing can seen in https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/40177f7bac4ab9615a32728154f6fd1fa6c8fcf9/src/tactic/portfolio/smt_strategic_solver.cpp#L123 .

Z3 makes a ["combined solver"](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/40177f7bac4ab9615a32728154f6fd1fa6c8fcf9/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp#L44) that is made of two different solvers. If push/pop are detected it uses the second solver, otherwise it uses the first solver.

The first solver from `mk_tactic2solver()` will be using the ["default" tactic](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/40177f7bac4ab9615a32728154f6fd1fa6c8fcf9/src/tactic/portfolio/default_tactic.cpp#L36) because you didn't specify a logic. This tactic probes the constraints to determine what the logic is and uses the appropriate tactic if the logic is recognised. In your case the probe will detect the logic is `QF_BV` and will use the `qfbv` tactic.

The second solver from `mk_solver_for_logic()` will make an incremental sat solver (`mk_inc_sat_solver()`) if you specify the logic as `QF_BV` otherwise the solver will be the general SMT solver (`mk_smt_solver()`) which in my experience is frequently slower than the solvers built from a tactic for a particular logic.

So to solve your problem.

* If you need incremental solving specify the logic with `(set-logic QF_BV)` at the beginning of the SMT-LIBv2 query.
* If you don't need incremental solving but don't know what the logic will be up front use `(reset)` to clear the assertion stack and avoid using push/pop.

Hope that helps.

@wintersteiger 
> I ran some tests with and without the segmented clause allocator. It does indeed increase performance, but only by a little bit, so for now I'm more inclined to keep it and fix it if necessary.

Thanks for taking a look. If the performance improvement is only slight my personal preference (of course I'm biased) would be to remove it or provide a way of disabling it so that it is possible to use the Address Sanitizer. I think this dynamic analysis instrumentation has proved its worth over the last few weeks by finding a bunch of bugs that have gone unnoticed in Z3 for quite a long time. Currently I can't use address sanitized builds of Z3 for my day to day development and testing because of this issue which is a shame.

> The assumption(s) about pointer values being aligned and from segments may not look pretty, but in practice they do help, but of course only on big instances where there is a huge number of pointers from few segments (and this is what happens on large SAT instances). Also, note that this part of the code is only enabled when _AMD64_ is set, i.e., not on all 64-bit platforms, only on AMD64-compliant ones, but not, for example, on IA64. So, fairly safe, but of course not completely.

My main concern isn't whether or not what Z3 does with pointers in the ``clause_allocator`` is pretty. My concern is that you're relying on specific implementation details of a system's ``malloc`` implementation. In general this isn't a good thing to do as you don't have control over this implementation which means your code is very fragile. Trying to use address sanitized builds has exposed this fragility.

Of course this concern needs to be balanced with the desire to make Z3 as performant as possible. I guess the Z3 team need to decide what is more important here, a slight performance increase at the cost of more fragile code, or slightly slower but less fragile code.

If you aren't willing to remove or fix this feature of the ``clause_allocator``, as a compromise would it be possible to provide a build time option to disable this optimization for AMD64? This option would be off by default (i.e. the optimization was enabled by default for AMD64) so Z3 would behave as before but enabling this option would allow me (and others) to use the address sanitizer. I realise this complicates the code even more but I think being able to use the address sanitizer is worth the hassle. You already seem to have special code that is intended for valgrind in Z3 (see #500) so there is already precedent for doing something like this.

On my side I'm happy to assist implementing the build system side of this (i.e. Telling the compiler about a new preprocessor define ``Z3_DISABLE_CLAUSE_ALLOCATOR_OPT``) if you like.

> I've never found rrule to be easy to use (that isn't to say it's not useful, just that I doubt my understanding of it), which is the main reason I wrote DateRange. I hear your point about timezones, some tests should be written that handle, for example, the crossover of EST to EDT (and back again). Though I'll also admit to being a huge proponent of timezones are UI concern and UTC should be the only thing your application deals with internally -- that said, plenty of people disagree or cannot do this for various reason, and being needlessly dogmatic just drives people away.

Time zones are meaningful in many instances when you are dealing with civil time, and can't be adequately represented by just working in UTC. Consider the case of some event that happens at 5PM EST every day (market close in NY?) or 8PM in PST (airing of a television show). Of course, some of this is solved by working in "naive" instead of working in UTC, but time zones are useful if you want some absolute information out of civil time (like you want to know the UTC time of the civil time events, or the total elapsed time between them).

My concerns with time zones and `DateRange` generally apply to to `rrule` as well, and my advice is generally to use naive zones or UTC depending on what you want (generally naive, because `rrule` is particularly useful for civil time), and then attach the time zones later. The way time zones are handled with `DateRange` could be the difference between a very simple implementation with many performance advantages over `rrule`  and it mapping directly onto `rrule` with very few simplifications possible that can't just be realized as enhancements to the underlying `rrule` class.

The separation was really just to get the tests for the `NonJournaledAsyncChainDB` passing. We can move everything back to the `JournalDB` if that turns out fast enough. However, since the `set` / `delete` API does not write to the underlying db until `.persist()` is called we need to decide how we want to handle places like this in `ChainDB`

```python
def persist_header(self, header: BlockHeader) -> Tuple[BlockHeader, ...]:
        """
        :returns: iterable of headers newly on the canonical chain
        """
        if header.parent_hash != GENESIS_PARENT_HASH and not self.header_exists(header.parent_hash):
            raise ParentNotFound(
                "Cannot persist block header ({}) with unknown parent ({})".format(
                    encode_hex(header.hash), encode_hex(header.parent_hash)))

        self.db.set(
            header.hash,
            rlp.encode(header),
        )

        if header.parent_hash == GENESIS_PARENT_HASH:
            score = header.difficulty
        else:
            score = self.get_score(header.parent_hash) + header.difficulty
        self.db.set(
            make_block_hash_to_score_lookup_key(header.hash),
            rlp.encode(score, sedes=rlp.sedes.big_endian_int))

        try:
            head_score = self.get_score(self.get_canonical_head().hash)
        except CanonicalHeadNotFound:
            new_headers = self._set_as_canonical_chain_head(header)
        else:
            if score > head_score:
                new_headers = self._set_as_canonical_chain_head(header)
            else:
                new_headers = tuple()

        return new_headers
```

The calls to `self.db.set(...)` won't write to the underlying db directly with the new `JournalDB`.

Here are some options:

1. Use a context manager API

```python
with db.record():
  db.set(...)
```

That will implicitly call `persist()` when the context is left.

2. Change `set` and `delete` to the following

```
def set(key: bytes, value:bytes, force_persist: boolean):
   ....
def delete(key: bytes, force_persist: boolean):
   ....
```

Then these places would call `self.db.set(x,y, True)`

3. Continue to use the raw database for the places where the `JournalDB` isn't needed

Personally, I think I'd just go with the second option for now.

Improved workaround for anyone who faces this issue in case it's not fixed soon:
```python
            if not ready:
                break   # timeout
            buf = os.read(self.fd, size-len(read))
            # read should always return some data as select reported it was
            # ready to read when we get to this point.
            toto = time.time() + 10
            while not buf:
                timeleft = toto - time.time()
                if timeleft < 0:
                    raise SerialException('device reports readiness to read but returned no data (device disconnected?)')
                buf = os.read(self.fd, size-len(read))
            read.extend(buf)
```
This gives a 10 second timeout (change at will depending on the case). The previous version had problems disconnecting the miniterm from the serial device.

Problem is, I can 't change the application, because it's delivered as a "baked" AppImage.
Also, select() has the "exception" file descriptor list, separate from the "read" file descriptor list.
I don't know what's actually going on in this case, because gtkterm works fine, as does "dd" into the serial port, as does custom C++ programs that talk to the serial port (using termios.)
So, to me, it seems like pyserial is not providing the same behavior as you'd expect from other programs.
Separately, I don't even understand why select() is needed. If you want timeouts on reading a serial port, that's what VTIME is for, and Python doesn't expose a globally multiplexed asynchronous I/O model like Node or Haskell.

I made some changes to [Integer Overflow](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/wiki/Integer-Overflow). Note that the Wiki is in git, and can be checkout out in git separately; so these changes can easily be reverted or you can see changes in detail. Although the git commit messages have some of the reason for the changes, I'll explain below.

In the particular situation we can list the EVM instructions which cause overflow. The specific instructions are `ADD`, `MUL`, and `EXP` and possibly `XADD` and `XMUL`. I think `ADDMON` and `MULMOD` don't. The reason for giving the instructions is that the specific instruction is listed in the error report that Mythril provides.

I think Mythril currently has a limitation that it only detects 2^256 kinds of overflows. If that's the case we should probably mention it, unless we are planning on addressing it soon. Here, I think you need to fold in information from static analysis. Static analysis can list bounds on specific instructions, and I think with that myth can use that information without much effort.

Here are some general markdown tips. If you add "solidity" after 3 backticks and github' markdown will color the code. See for example:

```
function run(unit256) public {
}
```

versus:

```solidity
function run(unit256) public {
}
```

When put the examples in a contract and compiled them, solidity tells me I need "public" or "private" in the function which is missing from the Ethereum examples. Rather than keep fidelity to the example I think it helpful to have code that doesn't compile with warnings.

Given that I was modifying the examples, I also pulled out the URL and made that clickable which I think is preferable. I know these are also listed in the references section at the bottom, but having the link near where that code is discussed I think helpful. Since the bottom section may have links to discussions that don't have examples or examples we use, that section should probably stay. Having duplicate links doesn't bother me.

And while on the references section, I put these in a markdown list rather than one per paragraph. I also added a blank line after section headings which I did not just because it might look nicer in editing, but I think there are some markdown renders that _require_ the blank line.

Feel free to push back on the changes or revert changes.

print(json.dumps(sys.path, indent=2))
[

  "/home/johnar/github/MonkeyType"
]

I did a little digging, at it seems that this is default behavior for python:

"projects/pathproblem" is added to sys.path by Python itself, not by PyCharm. See http://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.path : "As initialized upon program startup, the first item of this list, path[0], is the directory containing the script that was used to invoke the Python interpreter."

Because i was running /MonkeyType/monkeytype/cli.py as a script, it was adding the monkeytype directory to the path automatically.

For anyone else who may run into this path conflict while developing/debugging, my solution was just to add a run.py file to the top level directory of the project:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from monkeytype.cli import entry_point_main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    entry_point_main()
```

```
$ cli4 -v --raw per_page=90 page=0 /zones > /dev/null

I will add that maybe you intended it to try and upgrade from LDAP to LDAPS automatically even when ``use_ssl`` is ``False``. The problem with that in scenario encountered was that the LDAPS port was non functional because of certificate issues or something. In attempting LDAPS first, even though actually wanted only LDAP, the TLS failure would result in the whole thing failing and wasn't falling back to LDAP. So it was required to use LDAP and didn't want it trying LDAPS. The current setting interpretation means you can't say only try LDAP.

If you still want automatic upgrade, then maybe ``use_ssl`` should default to ``None`` meaning auto upgrade. Be able to be set to ``False`` to say no TLS and only try LDAP. And ``True`` means only LDAPS.
