I can't reproduce the error on my macbook, but I can using a linux VM, in
which I remove the system python, and install Python2.6 from sources.

I'm hesitant to write custom code for wheel installs to bypass this issue.
It's not difficult, but it's a very brittle solution: we'd wind up with a
parallel version of wheel install, which would need to track changes to the
standard wheel package. That's just begging from trouble down the line, in
order to continue to support a version of Python that's already
unsupported. (One of the problems I've had building an environment to
reproduce this bug locally is that it's hard to install a working 2.6
environment; even homebrew withdrew the formula for building one!)

   -Mark

On Thu, Jun 1, 2017 at 8:18 PM Dana Powers <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> I dug through a bit more and I'm confident this is actually hitting a bug
> deep within the interaction between wheel and zipfile on python2.6 .
> Specifically, when wheel installs a script it attempts to massage the
> #!python hashbang before processing the rest of the script. The code is
> here:
>
> https://bitbucket.org/pypa/wheel/src/0.29.0/wheel/install.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#install.py-346
>
> It does this by attempting to read a single line from the zipfile using
> zipfile.readline() . It then reads the remainder of the script using
> zipfile.read(). The problem is that the underlying zipfile library on
> python2.6 does not handle this sequence properly. You can test very easily
> by creating a zipfile with any script, open via z = zipfile.open(filename),
> then attempt to read a single line followed by the rest of the script w/
> readline() and read(). read() returns nothing! Very strange. This does not
> happen on python2.7, likely because the zipfile module was refactored to
> fix the underlying issue.
>
> I'm not sure what the best solution is here. First, I wonder if you can
> reproduce this zipfile bug? If so, I think the options are to (1) break pex
> installs of wheel files using python2.6, (2) write custom code to install
> the wheel package that does not have this bug, or (3) see if we can get a
> patch applied to upstream wheel.

I'd say you should be even more stricter with opt-in. Right now `pytest_fixture_setup` hook is too greedy: it always awaits async (generator) fixtures using the asyncio loop. But it shouldn't be the case when running tests for, say, `curio`. I think `pytest_fixture_setup` shouldn't touch async (generator) fixtures at all if they aren't explicitly marked with `@pytest.mark.asyncio`.

For instance, I face `Fatal Python error: GC object already tracked` crashes when trying to run tests for `curio` adapter while having both `pytest-curio` and `pytest-asyncio` installed, so that's a high priority issue for me.

I think you'd only *need* to call `python3 setup.py` if you wanted to use py3-only features in your `setup.py` file itself, which doesn't apply in our case.  I mean I'm no python packaging expert, but it's not like `python3 setup.py` is "compiling" the python sources for python3 or anything like that; it's just running the setuptools logic that copies over a bunch of python source into a zipfile (the wheel file) so it shouldn't really matter which version of python you use as long as it supports your `setup.py`.

I think we were just using it because it so happens that `setup.by bdist_wheel` will default the targeted python tag to the major version of the python binary you use to run it, so it functionally accomplished what we're now doing explicitly with `--python-tag`.

Re: pip3, yeah, I don't think we need to worry about that (I don't believe we're calling pip3 anywhere).

Hi Dave! The data type `-25` corresponds to `SQL_C_SBIGINT`, a signed 64-bit integer that turbodbc uses to query 64 bit fields. The [MS reference](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/odbc/reference/appendixes/c-data-types) has this to say about the datatypes:

> All drivers must support all C data types. This is required because all drivers must support all C types to which SQL types that they support can be converted, and all drivers support at least one character SQL type. Because the character SQL type can be converted to and from all C types, all drivers must support all C types.

This seems to be a problem with the Oracle ODBC driver that apparently does not support this particular data type, see the issues [here](https://forum.turi.com/discussion/1616/) and [there](http://sqlanywhere-forum.sap.com/questions/1260/how-do-i-get-past-oracleodbcinvalid-sql-data-type-25).

Maybe you can get around this by using an alternative or more modern ODBC driver?

I'm not knowledgeable on the ins and outs of this project, but I opened this because it didn't seem correct that letsencrypt's main configuration file was being left out. If letsencrypt intends to automatically update a cipher suite configuration over time, then leaving out options-ssl-nginx.conf (where the cipher suite configuration happens(?)) would break that functionality.

So I guess you could interpret this issue as "whatever you were intending to use options-ssl-nginx.conf for: it's not being used." I see there's some discussion at https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/2262#issuecomment-174060593 about some of this.

@eric-jj and @imzhenyu thanks for the clarifications. A few things:

1) Regarding Ray submit, if anyone has cycles to submit a github issue, with a short product requirement document that would be great. We definitely need help here, especially from people like you that understand well the use case.

2) Regarding resource management would **per-driver** resource management be good enough? As you know, in Ray you can have multiple drivers, so maybe this is enough; just associate a job or a user with a driver. The second question here is what kind of policies we would need to implement: fair sharing, priority, etc? Finally, again it would be great for someone to create a github issue a PRD.

3) Regarding eviction policy, there is no question that there are workloads for which LRU won't work well. However, it would be great to get more detailed examples. For instance, I'm still confused about this statement "... objects created by the long run job ... will be deleted because its' life time is longer than the objects' created by the small jobs." LRU will only evict the objects of the long jobs if they were not accessed recently; eviction is not really related to the object's lifetime. If you keep accessing the long job's objects, small jobs won't cause the eviction of these objects. 

Also, one question here. How useful would be an API like `ray.unsafe_evict(object_list)`, which will force the eviction of all objects in `object_list` form the object store. A variant would be  `ray.unsafe_evict(object_list, recursive=True)`, which will evict not only the objects in `object_list`, but also all objects on which these objects depend on. For instance, if `obj` is in `object_list`, then we evict not only `obj`, but also all "orphan" objects, i.e., objects whose descendants in the computation graph have been all removed. (I know that this is harder to use, but certainly is very flexible.)
 
An alternative would be to provide an API like `ray.pin(object_list)` which makes sure that none of the objects in `obj_list` will be removed by LRU.

Nice @nkeim! 
I think the new implementation now almost has the full functionality of the older.

About floating-point framenumbers: in #292 we concluded that framenumbers should be of integer type, as they refer to a discrete sequence of frames. This was also the rationale behind the new implementation of `Linker`: on each new frame, `next_level` is called.
`FindLinker` already is framenumber-aware, as it interprets `frame.frame_no`. The framenumbers are stored inside the `hash` objects (which are `TreeFinder` objects).

That said, the rest of `Linker` is (as far as I can see) dtype-agnostic. So if it suits you, I don't see any problem in leaving `frame` open to be of any type. The only thing is that `link_df` (and friends) have to be able to group and sort the dataframe correctly.

For the tests, I think it would make sense to adopt `frame` and `particle` columns that are of integer type. And (optionally) separately test with floating-point `frame` columns.

produce() is asynchronous, all it does is enqueue the message on an internal queue which is later (>= queue.buffering.max.ms) served by internal threads and sent to the broker (if a leader is available, else wait some more).

What this means in practice is that if you do:

    produce(1)
    produce(2)
    exit()

Then most likely neither message 1 or message 2 will have actually been sent to the broker, much less acknowledged by it.

Adding flush() before exiting will make the client wait for any outstanding messages to be delivered to the broker (and this will be around queue.buffering.max.ms, plus latency).
If you add flush() after each produce() call you are effectively implementing a sync producer (which you shouldn't, see here: https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/wiki/FAQ#why-is-there-no-sync-produce-interface).

Let me reconstruct your loop to be more performant:
```
conf = {'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092', 
             'queue.buffering.max.messages': 1000000, 
             'queue.buffering.max.ms' : 500,  ### <==== FIXME: this is fine for large files where the total send time will be above 500ms, but for small files it will add some delay - there is no harm in decreasing this value to something like 10 or 100 ms. On the other hand that may be offset by a lower batch.num.messages, but that is typically not the way to go. 
             'batch.num.messages': 50,   ### <=== FIXME: Why this low value? You typically don't need to alter the batch size.
             'default.topic.config': {'acks': 'all'}}

producer = confluent_kafka.Producer(**conf)

        try:
               
                fh = open(os.path.join("/home/samples/samples", queue.get()), "r")
                while True:
                    data = fh.read(10240)
                    if data == '':
                        fh.close()
                        break
                   try:
                        producer.produce(topic, value=data, callback=self.delivery_callback)
                        producer.poll(0)   # <=== not-FIXME: This is good, serve delivery report callbacks, but do note that it will not serve the delivery report of the message just produce()d since hasn't made it farther than the local queue at this point. It will serve previous message's delivery reports.
                   except BufferError as e:
                        print "Buffer full, waiting for free space on the queue"  # <==== not-FIXME: clarified this
                        producer.poll(10*1000)  # <==== FIXME: putting the poll() first to block until there is queue space available. This blocks for 10 seconds, but you should block for as long as makes sense for your application (preferably longer) since message delivery can take some time if there are temporary errors on the broker (e.g., leader failover).                  
                        producer.produce(topic, value=data, callback=self.delivery_callback)  # <=== FIXME: and now try again when there is hopefully some free space on the queue. 
                  #print "Waiting for %d deliveries\n" % len(producer)
                  #producer.flush()   # <==== FIXME: Bad, this implements a sync producer, very slow. remove it.
            except IOError as e:
                print "IO error"
            except ValueError:
                print "Conversion error"
            except:
                print "unexpected error"
                raise
            producer.flush()  # <==== FIXME: Put the flush() here instead to wait for any remaining delivery reports.
            queue.task_done()
```

I don't have this printer or write the same script, but maybe this extra information will help.

### Background
The standard code page system in ESC/POS is will work for single-byte encodings only, so you can switch the top 128 characters of the code page. This is not sufficient for Chinese, which is often implemented as a vendor-specific switch.

Eg, on the ZJ-5890T, there is a non-standard "GBK on" command. The developer then converts modern UTF-8 to GBK, which the printer accepts until "GBK off" is sent.

A Google search for your printer turned up a programming guide that does not mention CP936. Because the doc is in [google's cache](http://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?q=cache:qd-kmGtsAW4J:hanjindata.lgnas.com:10000/myweb/P3473-1/manual.pdf), I'll quote this command, which is enabled by default:

> ESC 8 n To select different dot matrix Chinese characters and print

So based on this, I think your printer expects GB2312 with no other settings.

### Test

So my hunch is that you can print just fine if you skip our encoder for single-byte code pages.

You didn't mention the interface that this printer has, but can you get text to your printer on the CLI?

```
$ echo "荣" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t GB2312 > /dev/usb/lp0
```

Might have to read the programming guide in some detail if this doesn't work.

Two things you will need to know next:

- how do I encode a string to GB2312 in python?
- how do I print raw text in python-escpos?

OK, I'm "confused" by this to say the least...

The relevant section in the SVG 1.1 specification - [8.3.9 The grammar for path data](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/paths.html#PathDataBNF) - clearly states a "flag" is supposed to be "0" or "1".

Now if we consider [F.6.2 Out-of-range parameters](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG11/single-page.html#implnote-ArcOutOfRangeParameters) we should also allow out of range values.

Problem: What do we do with path data like
```SVG
<path d="M280,120 h25 a25,25 0 10 0 -25,25 z" fill="red"/>
```
Now the "large arc flag" would be 10, but properly parsed it's actually 1 and sweep flag would be 0 and the path data therefore corrupted...

Should we consider "out-of-range" parameters to be arbitrarily limited to integers between -9 and 9, i.e. single digits? Or is the SVG spec simply inconsistent here?

So the fix above basically modified the signal handler to skip the SyncManager process while looping over all the children and terminating them, essentially leaving it to die when the master process sys.exit()s.

I think what's happening here is that the signal handlers for SIGINT/SIGTERM are being installed *before* any processes are being spawned and as such they are being inherited by all the children, including SyncManager. When e.g. systemd sends a TERM to all processes, the SyncManager process gets one as well and proceeds to immediately die, recreating the original issue.

I tried working around this, as a hack by wrapping the `Manager()`  instatiation as such:
```
-->     original_handler = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
-->     signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_IGN)

        # We do this weird process title swap around to get the sync manager
        # title correct for ps
        if setproctitle:
            oldproctitle = getproctitle()
            setproctitle('%s - SyncManager' % getproctitle())
        self.manager = multiprocessing.Manager()
        if setproctitle:
            setproctitle(oldproctitle)

-->     signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, original_handler)
```

This seems to have done the trick, although I think it's a bit of a hack as we set and unset the signal handler twice (it also doesn't cover SIGINT yet!). Moreover, it's not changing the behavior for any of the other children, but these probably need their own signal handler as well, so that they can exit themselves, but not loop over all processes like the parent one does.

In general, right now signal handlers are be installed in two places (server.py for HUP and the main binary for INT/TERM), one of which handles it in a special way (by the way of `signal_to_exception`) and that's a little messy. Properly fixing the code probably requires SIGTERM/INT handling in three different ways (looping over all children for the master process, ignoring it for the manager, just exiting for handlers/collectors), plus the HUP handling for config reloads. I've spent all of 10 minutes with the code so it's hard for me to pass any advice but intuitively it would make sense to me to centralize signal handling in one place and do it in one way (either functions or exceptions).

PS. Nothing very special with my config -- just one handler (Graphite) and four collectors (CPU, DiskUsage, Network, TCP).

fyi, this functionality now exists in a separate package:  https://github.com/mwcraig/ipyevents

This is a temporary solution that would allow capturing widget output until a dedicated output tab for all orphan output was implemented in jupyter-lab and/or jupyter-notebook.

The complexity is that the amount of output could be quite large (messages/errors can be generated for each widget change). 

This can be managed by either clearing output for each widget message or by having an adjustable-depth buffer.  The former would not work as the output widget utilizes the same widget messaging mechanism, resulting in the messages being cleared immediately by the update on the output widget. And the latter is a substantial amount of work/ux that may as well be redirected to the long-term solution.

> this moves in the direction of making a ginga widget easier. I think it might be better to add a Canvas widget for that (looks relatively straightforward).

@mwcraig, nice work!  This looks great and provides a couple of the most important event callbacks (if we could get keystrokes as well this would probably handle 95% of the needs!).  I agree with @maartenbreddels that this would work best as a new kind of widget, possibly just subclassed from `Image`.  Is that what you mean by a canvas widget ?

My suggestion for the ginga integration would be to follow the example for the [Tk back end](https://github.com/ejeschke/ginga/blob/master/ginga/tkw/ImageViewTk.py), which is similar in that it inherits from the PIL rendering base and also uses a `set_widget()` type method for connecting it to the image widget and setting up callbacks for the available events.  This approach would allow the most flexibility for the user to build up all the GUI with jupyter widgets and then just connect the ginga rendering (and now event mapping!) to the new widget with a simple `set_widget()` call at the end.

What this would mean is that we would add a proper `ImageViewJpy` *class* (to the module of the same name) and then an `ImageViewEvent` subclass to that.  You can see the progression in the Tk example.

'bandstructure_mode' now support 3 options:
1. False --> bandstructure not parsed at all (before False meant the same as parsing in uniform mode)
2. "line" --> line_mode = True in Vasprun.get_band_structure
3. "uniform" -->  line_mode = False in Vasprun.get_band_structure

why True="uniform":
1. ensure backward compatibility. Before, the default setting(bandstructure_mode=False) actually meant parsing banstructure using Vasprun without parsing the projected eigen values. I made it more explicit this time, with 'False' meaning no parsing at all.

2. ensure that bandstructure_mode can be set to either bool or string type, with 'True'/"uniform" being the default as before(no parsing of the projected eigen values). Before 'True' meant parsing projected eigen values and False meant uniform mode. In this PR, False ==> no parsing at all, True ==> it is parsed(just the eigen values) and for project eigen values one has to make that explicit by specifying bandstructure_mode = "line"

Similarly, adding 2 tuples to a new composite tuple with "+" works just

Yes, you could also keep redefining your original tuple. e.g.,

x = ("citation1",)
if something:

return x

But this is not as natural as extending lists which are meant to be
mutable. Also, I know this seems like a small thing, but the need for a
comma for single-element tuples is actually pretty annoying. I know I've
fixed the missing comma for other people's functions in matminer before

together lots of citations and implementors from a bunch of different
featurizers, I don't think the order really matters anymore so sets are
fine. But for a single featurizer, I think it's nice to retain an ordering
to the implementors and maybe even the citations. e.g., for the
MiedemaFeaturizer, Alireza helped implement a little bit but Qi is really
the "boss". So I think it's nice if Qi's name can always come first so
people know she is the main contact.

Hi @benjaoming thanks for your work so far on this.

For 0.6 (need to do it this week) I think we need a much simpler strategy with zero user interaction - something that works for our non-command-line users also.

The goal at this point should be to _not_ expose any of this backup functionality to the end-user. The only time they'd need to know it's happening is if something goes horribly wrong - we'll quickly learn about it, and then we work with them closely to show them how to downgrade.

For now, can we simply do a backup without any user interaction whenever the Kolibri version changes, and only keep the most recent backup? This will give us an emergency escape hatch for all users (including those using Windows, Android, etc) but in all likelihood it will be one we'll never need to exercise.

It seems like implementing the jsonapi spec by composing `marshmallow_jsonapi` out of pure marshmallow fields and schemas would remove the need for `@pre/post_*` glue.

> OK... so I'd just define the Great3Reject type in great3_reject.py in examples/great3/ ? And the idea is that this class would be initialized with all the basic numbers that define the cuts, and then when it's called it would take a stamp as an input and return a boolean value?

You can see an example custom module that uses hsm to measure the of a drawn object [here](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/%23654/examples/des/hsm_shape.py).  I use it in a [DES example script](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/%23654/examples/des/meds.yaml) for the output truth values of the PSF shape.  It's a bit gratuitous, but it's intended to serve as an example of the kind of thing we're doing here with writing custom types outside of GalSim.

Here is a strawman of the code in great3_reject.py:
```
def Great3Reject(config, base, value_type):
    """See if a drawn postage stamp should be rejected based on Great3 cuts.
    """
    stamp = base['current_stamp']
    gal = base['gal']['current_val']
    psf = base['gal']['current_val']

    reject = False

    # Check the flux
    flux = numpy.sum(stamp.array)
    if flux < 0.99 * gal.flux:
        reject = True

     # ... Other checks

    return reject


galsim.config.RegisterValueType('Great3Reject', Great3Reject, [ bool ])
```

from gcloud import pubsub

@ZuluPro I understand your concerns. We have the same ones. We often rebase un-published work after a main fork updates.

I just wanted to check that there wasn't a reason you were completely blocked, and that you know how to work around _not_ having a PR merged into the main fork.

Especially the `-D` flag of git branch doesn't behave as the common posix flag parsing would suggest. As the git command wrapper as to make certain assumptions, the issue you see is expected.

However, in these cases one can pass the arguments explicitly, such as in this example:
```python
repo.git.branch('-D', 'somebranch')
```

Please also note that the usage of `repo.git` is only advised as it's considered un-pythonic and bare-bone. A nicer way of achieving the branch creation and deletion presented here is the following:
```python
foo = repo.create_head('foo')
assert foo.is_valid()
repo.delete_head(foo)
assert not foo.is_valid()
```

One will have to spread these efforts among GitDB and SMMap as well. Both should have mechanisms to release resources, but I doubt this works properly.

For now the best way to workaround these resource related issues is to use a `GitCmdObjectDB`, at least when working with non-ref/object data.
